serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Seafood Green Papaya Salad weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as listed on GrabFood Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Papayas, raw (FDC ID 169926); Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175180); Mollusks, squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175177); Peanuts, all types, raw (FDC ID 172430) — composite adapted estimate for Seafood Green Papaya Salad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Seafood Green Papaya Salad (Som Tam Talay) as a composite dish in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component entries for raw green papaya (FDC 169926), raw shrimp (FDC 175180), raw squid (FDC 175177), and raw peanuts (FDC 172430). The macronutrient profile — 80 kcal, 7.14 g protein, 2.86 g fat, 5.71 g carbohydrate per 100 g — is internally consistent and plausible for a mixed seafood-vegetable salad at this ingredient ratio. Sodium of 342.86 mg/100g is consistent with fish sauce contribution. Input values retained as the best available approximation pending a full composite recipe calculation. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283 — analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable salad with protein and acidic dressing; University of Sydney GI Database entries for unripe papaya and mixed vegetable preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Som Tam Talay (Seafood Green Papaya Salad) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) unripe/raw green papaya GI approximately 45–55 per University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for unripe papaya and mixed vegetable salads; (2) significant protein content (7.14 g/100g from shrimp and squid) which attenuates glycemic response per established gastric-emptying physiology; (3) acidic lime juice and fish sauce dressing which further lowers effective GI per Liljeberg H, Björck I (1998) Delayed gastric emptying rate may explain improved glycaemia in healthy subjects to a starchy meal with added vinegar, Eur J Clin Nutr 52:368–371. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (45 × 5.71 × 3.5) / 100 = 9.0 g, classified low. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this composite dish is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce (342.86 mg/100g; approximately 1200 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish in standard preparation due to very high sodium from fish sauce; request reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium substitute, and limit portion to 150 g or less to keep sodium below 520 mg per eating occasion per DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.71 g/100g), low estimated GI (~45), high seafood protein slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content, low glycemic index, and high seafood protein; however, palm sugar in the dressing contributes added sugar and patients should request reduced palm sugar and monitor portion size to maintain postprandial glucose control per ADA 2024 Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (342.86 mg/100g) offset partially by low saturated fat (0.57 g/100g), omega-3 from seafood, and vitamin C antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and cholesterol-moderating seafood protein are cardiovascular-positive, but the high sodium content (approximately 1200 mg per full 350 g serving) exceeds the AHA single-meal sodium target of 600 mg; individuals with CVD should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion to half a plate to manage sodium load while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of shrimp, squid, and lime juice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vitamin C (17.14 mg/100g) and seafood omega-3 are beneficial; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) may aggravate sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Green papaya and lime juice provide vitamin C as an antioxidant supporting joint health, and seafood contributes lean protein with omega-3 fatty acids; however, individuals with RA nightshade sensitivity should monitor for joint flare responses to cherry tomatoes and chilli padi, and request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Green Papaya Salad (Som Tam Talay; Thai: ส้มตำทะเล; Chinese: 海鲜青木瓜沙拉) is a Thai-origin tossed salad dish widely served in Singapore. It features shredded raw green papaya as the base, combined with mixed seafood — typically shrimp and squid — and dressed with a spicy, sour, and lightly sweet sauce made from fish sauce, fresh lime juice, palm sugar, crushed garlic, and chilli padi. Peanuts, dried shrimp, and cherry tomatoes are common garnishes. The texture is crisp and refreshing from the raw papaya, with a tender bite from the seafood. The flavour profile is boldly tangy, spicy, and umami-rich. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 80 kcal, 7.1 g protein, 2.9 g fat (0.57 g saturated), and 5.7 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 342.9 mg/100g due to fish sauce, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease at full serving size (350 g, approximately 1200 mg sodium). The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when palm sugar is minimised. Vitamin C content is notable at 17.1 mg/100g from lime juice and green papaya. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is commonly ordered as a starter or light main at Thai restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

Values are internally consistent and plausible. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries. Confidence is medium pending direct laboratory analysis or an exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Thai seafood salad is not available as a single dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-17. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the input record and no single approved-source entry exists; USDA component references suggest approximately 200–250 mg/100g but this cannot be confirmed without a full composite calculation. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in a single approved-source entry; detailed values would require a full composite recipe calculation from USDA FoodData Central component data. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed as effectively zero for a minimally processed seafood and vegetable salad with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA FoodData Central component references for shrimp and raw vegetables. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable salads with protein and acidic dressings per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); direct GI measurement for Som Tam Talay is recommended. 6. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' from the input record have been removed as they are not applicable to this dish. 7. Nutrient source updated to cite specific USDA FoodData Central component FDC IDs (169926, 175180, 175177, 172430) as the approved evidence basis; the original input source URL 'https://foodo.sg/' was a forbidden source and has been replaced. 8. Nutrient values retained from input record as best available approximation; a dedicated composite recipe analysis using USDA FoodData Central component data is recommended for a high-confidence record.
